explanation: TRUE POSITIVE. The `withdrawByID` function uses a two-loop pattern: Loop 1 (lines 129-139) calculates the total withdrawal amount by iterating over user-supplied `_ids`, and Loop 2 (lines 142-158) deletes records after the transfer at line 141. There is no duplicate ID check. When duplicate non-zero IDs are passed (e.g., `[5, 5, 5]`), Loop 1 counts the same record's amount multiple times, inflating the total. The inflated amount is transferred to the caller. In Loop 2, the first occurrence successfully calls `delRecord`, which clears `id2addr[_id]` to `address(0)`. Subsequent duplicate iterations call `getRecordByID` which returns an empty struct (addr=0), causing the condition `record.addr == msg.sender` to fail silently — no revert. The attacker thus receives N times their actual balance for N duplicates of the same ID. The function is `public` with no access control, so any user with a valid unlocked record can exploit this to drain contract funds. Note: ID 0 duplicates would revert due to Solidity 0.8 underflow protection on `balances[msg.sender] -= temp`, but non-zero ID duplicates are fully exploitable.
